coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of heterostructures based on S-doped polymeric carbon nitride and carbon dots for enhancing photocatalytic H2 production
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Campos, Rafael Alves
Mota, Adnaildo Miranda
Mercante, Luiza Amim
Silva, Luciana Almeida
coredata.dc:description: Polymeric carbon nitride (PCN) is a promising metal-free visible-light-driven material for solar hydrogen production because of its photocatalytic response to visible light and easy fabrication. However, its photocatalytic activity is limited due to low absorption in a wide solar spectrum, poor charge separation efficiency, and low charge carrier mobility. Herein, we present a strategy to address these issues by combining sulfur-doped PCN (SPCN) with carbon dots (CDs). To this end, we developed a straightforward synthetic procedure based on the calcination of thiourea and small amounts of citric acid. The presence of citric acid in the reaction medium led to the formation of carbon dots (CD) on the surface of the semiconductor material, enhancing light absorption and improving the photocatalytic production of H2. Different amounts of citric acid were used to explore the influence of the CDs on the hydrogen production of SPCN. The obtained materials were thermally exfoliated to increase the surface area and then characterized using XRD, FT-IR, DRS, SEM, and BET. The optimal SPCNCD heterostructure, obtained with 60 mg of citric acid, exhibited improved H2 production activity of 1756 μmol g−1 h−1 of H2 over six hours when irradiated with a solar simulator due to a narrower band gap. Our study provides valuable insights for designing metal-free heterostructures for photocatalysis applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymeric carbon nitride
Sulfur doping
Carbon dots
Heterostructure
Photocatalysis
Hydrogen production

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the Brazilian research funding agencies Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Cient\u00EDfico e Tecnol\u00F3gico (CNPq, Grant Numbers: 407968/2022\u20139 and 405819/2022\u20136) and Coordena\u00E7\u00E3o de Aperfei\u00E7oamento de Pessoal de N\u00EDvel Superior (CAPES, Finance Code 001) for financial support. The authors also thank LAMUME \u2013 UFBA for TEM analysis.

The authors acknowledge the Brazilian research funding agencies Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Cient\u00EDfico e Tecnol\u00F3gico (CNPq, Grant Numbers: 407968/2022-9 and 405819/2022-6 ) and Coordena\u00E7\u00E3o de Aperfei\u00E7oamento de Pessoal de N\u00EDvel Superior (CAPES, Finance Code 001 ) for financial support. The authors also thank LAMUME \u2013 UFBA for TEM analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the (a) as-prepared and (b) exfoliated samples.
SEM images of (a) as-prepared and (b) exfoliated SPCNCD60. (c) TEM and (d) HRTEM images of exfoliated SPCNCD60 (CDs are marked in yellow circles).
FTIR spectra of the (a) as-prepared and (b) exfoliated samples.
DRS spectra of the (a) as-prepared and (b) exfoliated samples. Tauc plot of the (c) as-prepared and (d) exfoliated samples.
(a) Kinetic curves of hydrogen production for all exfoliated SPCNCD heterostructures. (b) Hydrogen production rate in function of CA amount compared with the respective band gaps.

Kinetic curves of hydrogen production for SPCNCD60-ex: (a) with different photocatalyst concentrations, (b) in different platinum concentrations (0.10 g L−1 of photocatalyst), (c) long-term reaction (0.10 g L−1 of photocatalyst and 3 % Pt), and (d) recycle test (0.50 g L−1 of photocatalyst and 3 % Pt).
Schematic illustration to prepare exfoliated SPCNCD heterostructures.
Scheme of a possible mechanism for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution over exfoliated SPCNCD heterostructure with Pt as a cocatalyst.
Textural properties of the SPCNCD heterostructures before and after exfoliation.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: SBET: Brunauer, Emmett and Teller Surface Area; DP: pore diameter.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Band gap energies for as-prepared and exfoliated CD-SPCN heterostructures.

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of heterostructures based on S-doped polymeric carbon nitride and carbon dots for enhancing photocatalytic H2 production

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bInstituto Nacional de Ciência e Tecnologia, INCT, de Energia e Ambiente, Universidade Federal da Bahia, 40170-290 Salvador, BA, Brazil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polymeric carbon nitride (PCN) is a promising metal-free visible-light-driven material for solar hydrogen production because of its photocatalytic response to visible light and easy fabrication. However, its photocatalytic activity is limited due to low absorption in a wide solar spectrum, poor charge separation efficiency, and low charge carrier mobility. Herein, we present a strategy to address these issues by combining sulfur-doped PCN (SPCN) with carbon dots (CDs). To this end, we developed a straightforward synthetic procedure based on the calcination of thiourea and small amounts of citric acid. The presence of citric acid in the reaction medium led to the formation of carbon dots (CD) on the surface of the semiconductor material, enhancing light absorption and improving the photocatalytic production of H2. Different amounts of citric acid were used to explore the influence of the CDs on the hydrogen production of SPCN. The obtained materials were thermally exfoliated to increase the surface area and then characterized using XRD, FT-IR, DRS, SEM, and BET. The optimal SPCNCD heterostructure, obtained with 60 mg of citric acid, exhibited improved H2 production activity of 1756 μmol g−1 h−1 of H2 over six hours when irradiated with a solar simulator due to a narrower band gap. Our study provides valuable insights for designing metal-free heterostructures for photocatalysis applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The carbon footprint related to hydrogen generation is medium to high in processes that use natural gas and hydrocarbons as feedstock [1–5]. To address these issues, more sustainable alternatives for hydrogen production are sought. In this way, photocatalytic water splitting is a potential approach for clean hydrogen production using sunlight-assisted semiconductors [6–9].

In this regard, carbon nitride-based materials have emerged as promising visible-light photocatalysts owing to their environmentally friendly nature and great potential for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution in both acidic and basic media [10–12]. For instance, polymeric carbon nitride (PCN), a sunlight-active semiconductor consisting of heptazine (C6N7) unis structures, has attracted increasing attention because of its good stability, chemical tunability, and low cost [13,14].

Despite carbon nitride-based materials being widely considered potential metal-free semiconductors for solar energy conversion, the pristine PCN has some drawbacks for application in photocatalytic hydrogen production, such as a small specific surface area, limited light absorption (band gap of 2.7 eV), fast recombination of electron-hole pairs, and poor conductivity [15,16]. Several strategies are usually applied to achieve desirable properties for PCN, such as thermal exfoliation to increase the surface area [10], doping to tune the electronic and photonic properties [17], and heterostructure formation [13,18]. For instance, our previous work demonstrated that doping PCN with sulfur (SPCN) reduced band gap energy and decreased the photoluminescence intensity, indicating suppression of charge recombination [19].

Combining PCN with other materials to form heterostructures has also proven to be an efficient strategy to enhance its photocatalytic activity. For instance, combining PCN and carbon dots results in a metal-free heterostructure with high-performance photocatalytic hydrogen production driven by visible light [16,20–24]. Carbon dots (CDs) are monodisperse graphite particles <10 nm in diameter that exhibit photoluminescence, help to broaden visible light absorption, retard charge recombination and promote photoelectrons transport [16,20]. PCNCDs heterostructures were first prepared by calcinating CDs in the presence of urea [20]. Later, PCNCDs heterostructures were synthesized by directly combining urea and small amounts of CD's precursors, such as glucose, citric acid, and dimethylformamide (DMF), in a one-pot synthesis [21,25,26]. Despite these previous reports, the combination of CDs with S-doped PCN is generally constructed by two-step procedures, where the CDs were prepared ex-situ and then heat treated with the precursor of SPCN to give rise to SPCNCD heterostructures [27–31].

Inspired by the abovementioned strategies, we propose a one-pot synthesis of SPCNCD heterostructures with in-situ CD formation during the thermal polycondensation of thiourea in the presence of small amounts of citric acid (CDs precursor), which allows the preparation of an earth-abundant photocatalyst via a simple, low-cost, and environmentally friendly route. Usually, the thermal polycondensation method results in materials with low surface area. In this way, the direct formation of SPCNCD heterostructures with CDs embedded in a sulfur-doped carbon nitride matrix followed by thermal exfoliation improved photocatalytic efficiency under solar irradiation by enhancing light absorption range and greater exposure of active sites due to enhancing surface area. Moreover, we systematically investigated the influence of the amount of citric acid precursor on the band gap of the composite material to achieve the highest hydrogen production rate. These insights can contribute to advancing research on photocatalytic materials for sustainable hydrogen production.

The chemicals used in the synthesis and photocatalytic tests were of analytical grade, and all of them were used as purchased. Urea and thiourea were purchased from Neon. Citric acid was purchased from Exodo Cientifica. The solution of 8wt.% hexachloroplatinic acid hexahydrate was purchased from Sigma, and triethanolamine (TEOA) was purchased from Merk.

S-doped PCNCD heterostructures (SPCNCD) were synthesized by a one-pot high-temperature polymerization method. 40 g of thiourea were combined with 20, 60, 80, or 120 mg of citric acid (carbon dots precursor) in a covered crucible and calcined at 600 °C for three hours with a heating rate of 1 °C min−1. The solids produced were labeled SPCNCD20, SPCNCD60, SPCNCD80, and SPCNCD120. As references, pristine PCN and SPCN were also prepared by heating 50 g of urea and thiourea, respectively, at the same conditions described above.

After grinding, 500 mg of each SPCNCD sample was placed in a boat-shaped crucible and treated at 500 °C for three hours with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1. The materials were cooled, washed three times with ultrapure water to remove any impurities, and dried in an oven for 12 h at 70 °C, giving rise to the samples SPCNCD20-ex, SPCNCD60-ex, SPCNCD80-ex, and SPCNCD120-ex. We followed the same procedure for SPCN exfoliation, which yielded the sample SPCN-ex.
Scheme 1 illustrates the whole process of preparing the exfoliated S-doped PCNCD heterostructures.

The solids were characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (Shimadzu XRD6000) with a scanning rate of 2° 2θ min−1, in the range 2θ of 5–80°, using CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) and graphite monochromator, at 40 kV and 30 mA. The morphology was assessed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM; Hitachi S3400N® 100 kV). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained using a JOEL JEM 2800 microscope operated with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared by attenuated total reflection (ATR-FTIR) spectra were recorded on a spectrometer Shimadzu IRAffinity using 32 spectral scans accumulation of 4000 a 400 cm−1 with 4 cm−1 resolution. The BET surface areas and average pore sizes were calculated from the treatment of N2 adsorption isotherms collected at 77 K after vacuum pretreating the samples at 323 K for 12 h in a Micromeritics ASAP2020 automated sorptometer. Diffuse reflectance spectra in the UV–vis region were recorded on a Thermo Evolution 600 spectrometer.

The bandgap energies were determined using diffuse reflectance data, applying the Tauc method [32]. The Tauc equation can be expressed as:
(1)
α h ν = A
( h ν −
E g
)
n
,
Where α is the absorption coefficient, hν is the photon energy, Eg is the bandgap energy, n = 1/2 for direct transition, and A is a proportional constant. When the reflectance is measured instead of absorption, it can be converted into an absorption coefficient based on the Kubelka Munk (K-M) function. Thus, Eq. (1) changes and can be expressed as Eq. (2) (for more details, please see elsewhere [32]):
(2)
F
(
R ∞
)
h ν = A
( h ν −
E g
)
1 / 2
,
R∞ is the reflectance of the sample with infinite thickness (>2mm) since the contribution of the sample holder is negligible. Thus, the plot of [F(R∞ )hν]2 versus hν leads to an extrapolation that estimates the value of the bandgap by fitting a straight line to the linear segment to intersect the hν-axis [32].

Photocatalytic experiments were carried out with the assistance of a solar simulator LCS-100 94011A Oriel Instruments with an AM1.5 G spectral correction filter. In a typical experiment for each target solid sample, 80 mg was dispersed in 80 mL of 10 % triethanolamine-aqueous solution containing 20 μL of 8 % wt. H2[PtCl6].6H2O. Triethanolamine was used as a hole scavenger, while hexachloroplatinic acid is the Pt0 precursor that acts as a cocatalyst in hydrogen production [33]. The reactor was purged with argon before irradiation to eliminate oxygen gas. The reactor with a total volume of 170 mL has argon gas inlet/outlet tubes to collect and transfer gaseous products to the analytical system. A gas chromatograph (SHIMADZU, GC2014) measured hydrogen gas evolution with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and argon as the carrier gas. A gas pump (SCHWARZER, model PN SP625EC) injected the gaseous products into the GC system. A 5 % H2 standard diluted in argon was injected before each experiment to quantify the hydrogen produced during the reaction.

The crystal structure of pristine PCN, sulfur-doped PCN (SPCN), and SPCNCD heterostructures before and after exfoliation was assessed by X-ray diffraction and comparison with the literature [13,18]. The XRD patterns for all samples are compatible with the PCN structure (Fig. 1 ). The peak assigned to in-plane repeats between heptazine (C6N7) units are centered at 12.8° (Fig. 1a) and 12.7° (Fig. 1b) for the samples before and after exfoliation, respectively. The peak shift toward the lower angle indicates the increased distance between heptazine units in the plane due to the exfoliation. The sharpest peak, at 27.7° for both as-prepared (Fig. 1a) and exfoliated samples (Fig. 1b), refers to the (002) plane, related to the π-π stacking motif of the heptazines on top of each other [13]. In this case, there is no peak shift, which means that the exfoliation process moves away the heptazine rings in the plane but keeps the distance between the stacked heptazine rings.

The morphology of SPCNCD heterostructures before and after exfoliation was assessed using SEM. The SEM images of a select sample (Fig. 2 ) demonstrate a compact structure for the as-prepared material, indicating low surface area and porosity (Fig. 2a). In contrast, after the exfoliation, the sample shows an expanded lamellar structure (Fig. 2b), which suggests an increase in surface area. Moreover, the TEM image of the exfoliated material illustrates a thin, transparent SPCN sheet with a silk-like nanostructure morphology. The HRTEM image (Fig. 2d) illustrated that nanosized CDs (marked by yellow circles) were uniformly distributed on the SPCN surface, indicating the formation of heterojunctions between CDs and the SPCN matrix. This result evidences the efficiency of the proposed method in obtaining SPCNCD heterostructures in situ from the carbonization of small amounts of citric acid during the polycondensation of thiourea.

The increase in surface area was confirmed by BET analysis (Table 1 ). As shown, the BET specific surface area (SBET) increased from 25.35 – 34.80 m2 g−1 to 86.87 – 120.82 m2 g−1 after the exfoliation. The high SBET is beneficial for exposing more active sites and improving photocatalytic activity. Furthermore, all materials present a mesoporous structure, with an average pore size of 11 nm after the thermal exfoliation process.

The molecular structure of PCN-based materials was analyzed by FTIR spectra (Fig. 3 ). The absorption band in the 3600–3200 cm−1 range is characteristic of the stretching of amine groups. The bands at 1200 - 1600 cm−1 are indicative of the stretching modes of the triazine (C6N7) units, and the band at 810 cm−1 is associated with the heptazine ring out-of-plane twisted vibration mode [34–36]. All samples have almost identical characteristic peaks, indicating that neither the doping of S atoms nor the introduction of CDs has altered the molecular structure of PCN, which may be due to the relatively low sulfur and CD amounts in the SPCNCD heterostructures.

The color of the obtained products gradually changed from light yellow to brown with the increase of the citric acid and thiourea mass ratio (inset Fig. 4 a and b). Consequently, the UV–vis spectra of the as-prepared materials show a clear trend of extending the absorption to longer wavelengths with increased citric acid in the start mixture (Fig. 4a). After thermal exfoliation, a blue shift is observed in all cases, which can be assigned to the particle size decrease caused by thermal exfoliation.

The SPCNCD heterostructures' band gap before and after exfoliation was estimated from the Tauc plot [32] considering direct band gap materials [37]. The results showed that the increasing citric acid mass generally contributed to the band gap narrowing for as-prepared materials (Table 2 ), indicating the successful formation of CDs [16]. However, the exfoliation of bulk SPCN into nanosheets results in the quantum confinement effect caused by the decrease in particle size [38]. This effect shifts the conduction and valence band edges in opposite directions, causing a widening band gap compared to non-exfoliated samples. The band gap values of exfoliated samples decrease with the increase of citric acid amount until 60 mg, following the same tendency as the non-exfoliated samples. After that, the band gap increased as the amount of citric acid increased, which was counter to the tendency of the non-exfoliated samples. High concentrations of citric acid can lead to the formation of CDs with high amounts of oxygen functional groups, which could be more easily degraded [39] during the thermal exfoliation process. The lower concentration of CDs after exfoliation may explain the widening bandgap when high concentrations of citric acid are used.

The exfoliated samples were tested in sunlight-assisted photocatalytic hydrogen production (Fig. 5 a) in 10 % TEOA and 1 % Pt. All samples were active for photocatalytic hydrogen production and were more active than pure SPCN. The influence of the amount of citric acid (precursor of the carbon dots) can be visualized in Fig. 5b (blue curve). As the amount of carbon dots precursor increases, the hydrogen production rate also increases to a limit of 60 mg, reaching a hydrogen evolution rate of 694 μmol g−1 h−1. After that, a slight decrease in hydrogen production is observed up to 80 mg, followed by a sharp drop to 120 mg. Fig. 5b (red curves) also illustrates the dependence of the hydrogen evolution rate on the band gap energies. As the band gap decreases, the hydrogen production rate increases up to the limit of 2.65 eV, corresponding to the CA mass of 60 mg. For larger amounts of CA, the band gap widens with a consequent decrease in the H2 evolution rate. Therefore, the mass of 60 mg is the right amount of citric acid for preparing the exfoliated SPCNCD heterostructure to apply in hydrogen production.

To maximize hydrogen production, the most photoactive sample, SPCNCD60-ex, was chosen to assess the effect of reaction parameters, such as the concentration of photocatalyst and cocatalyst. Fig. 6 a displays the kinetic curves for hydrogen production using photocatalyst concentrations ranging from 0.1 g L−1 to 1.0 g L−1. The hydrogen production rate is the maximum for the lowest concentration of the photocatalyst, reaching 1360 μmol g−1 h−1. So, the concentration of 0.1 g L−1 was fixed to evaluate the effect of cocatalyst concentration (1 % and 3 % Pt). One can see in Fig. 6b that increasing Pt concentration from 1 % to 3 % increases the hydrogen production rate, reaching a maximum of 1756 μmol g−1 h−1.

A long-term reaction was performed for SPCNCD60-ex in optimized condition (0.10 g L−1 of photocatalyst and 3 % Pt), and the photocatalyst continuously produced hydrogen over 24 h (Fig 6c). Further, three photocatalytic cycles were performed using the same photocatalyst and a fresh TEOA solution for each cycle to evaluate the reuse of the photocatalysts. In the first photocatalytic cycle, a fresh photocatalyst was used, collected after irradiation, washed with deionized water, dried, and used in the next cycle. For this test, the photocatalyst concentration used was 0.5 g L−1, which allowed the recovery of the solid for the next cycles. The results are shown in Fig. 6d, noting that the hydrogen production profiles are virtually identical for the three cycles. Such results demonstrate the high performance and stability of the SPCNCD heterostructure in the photocatalytic production of hydrogen.

According to Yuan et al. [29], sulfur doping and CDs enable carbon nitride to obtain higher electron-hole separation efficiency. The electrons may migrate from SPCN to CDs to achieve Fermi-level (EF) equilibrium. In the sequence, electrons could be injected into the Pt cocatalyst from the CDs driven by the most negative EF to promote H + reduction to molecular hydrogen [16]. At the same time, the photogenerated holes are transferred to TEOA, resulting in its oxidation. A scheme depicting a possible photocatalytic mechanism is illustrated in Scheme 2 .

In summary, SPCNCD heterostructures were synthesized through a simple one-step thermal polycondensation method for photocatalytic hydrogen production. By optimizing the mass of citric acid precursor during the synthesis procedure, the obtained SPCNCD heterostructure exhibited improved photocatalytic hydrogen production activity compared to bare SPCN. The introduction of CDs reduced the bandgap, and the thermal exfoliation treatment augmented the specific surface area, enhancing the photocatalytic activity. The highest hydrogen production rate (694 μmol g−1 h−1) was achieved for the SPCNCD60-ex heterostructure, reaching 1756 μmol g−1 h−1 after optimization of reaction parameters. This study presents an alternative approach to preparing metal-free heterostructured photocatalysts for photocatalytic hydrogen production.

Rafael Alves Campos: Writing – original draft, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Adnaildo Miranda Mota: Methodology. Luiza Amim Mercante: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Data curation. Luciana Almeida Silva: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the Brazilian research funding agencies Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico (CNPq, Grant Numbers: 407968/2022–9 and 405819/2022–6) and Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior (CAPES, Finance Code 001) for financial support. The authors also thank LAMUME – UFBA for TEM analysis.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optimizing crystallinity and defects in carbon nitride for enhanced visible-light photocatalytic hydrogen production
